subject: concept:paradox-of-virality
quote: This gap between people's stated preferences and behavior is called the paradox of virality, or the idea that widely shared content is not necessarily widely liked.
rationale: Paper names and operationally defines the paradox of virality as a dedicated concept — direct evidentiary engagement.

subject: concept:paradox-of-virality
quote: even though people are aware that negative and high-arousal content goes "viral" on social media, the vast majority of people across the political spectrum reported that they do not want this to be the case.
rationale: What spreads diverges from receiver preference, locating the cause in the selection gate not receiver demand — supports selection-primary.

subject: concept:superspreader-dynamics
quote: a small number of passionate "superspreaders" may be disproportionately responsible for sharing it. One estimate suggests that about 0.1% of Twitter users share about 80% of misinformation on Twitter.
rationale: Paper develops superspreader dynamics with quantitative estimates — a few high-degree nodes drive disproportionate spread.

subject: concept:superspreader-dynamics
quote: the online world has much larger social networks. The sheer size of online networks allows for "superspreader" dynamics that could not exist in the offline world.
rationale: Superspreader dynamics are a network-size effect with no offline equivalent at scale — ties the concept to the network-size bound.

subject: concept:algorithm-as-selection-engine
quote: the fact that algorithms amplify attention-grabbing content means that they can also amplify high-arousal negativity and other forms of content people do not want to see but is nonetheless engaging.
rationale: Paper treats recommendation algorithms as an amplification/selection mechanism distinct from transport — substantive engagement.

subject: concept:algorithm-as-selection-engine
quote: Social media companies can prioritize people's stated preferences to design better algorithms.
rationale: Algorithm selection criteria are tunable design choices — supports that the gate's criteria are downstream of medium-level decisions.

subject: concept:algorithm-as-selection-engine
quote: The modern spread of information is facilitated by algorithms that show people content they are likely to engage with. Facebook has argued that their news feed ranking algorithm shows people what they "want to see"
rationale: Algorithms select on engagement, an inverse-to-quality criterion — relates the selection engine to the spread-vs-quality dynamic.

subject: concept:epistemic-vigilance
quote: others suggest that our tendency to ignore and resist information is a feature of our "mental immune systems," expanding on the metaphor further.
rationale: Paper explicitly invokes "mental immune systems" and human resistance to information — direct engagement with epistemic vigilance.

subject: concept:epistemic-vigilance
quote: the virus metaphor ignores human agency, since people do not passively consume information, but interpret, transform, or ignore information.
rationale: Receiver vigilance complicates any pure low-friction-spread model: easy spread is gated by cognitive defenses, not just compressed form.

subject: concept:meme-ecosystem-competition
quote: In a crowded information environment in which many different pieces of information are competing for our attention (such as a social media news feed)
rationale: Paper frames spread as competition among many pieces of information for finite attention — the ecosystem-competition premise.

subject: concept:meme-ecosystem-competition
quote: just as a crowded room is conducive to the spread of a virus, some environments (e.g., some social media platforms) might also be conducive to the spread of certain types of information
rationale: Spread is relative to the competing environment, not intrinsic — weakly backs selection as the determinant within a crowded ecosystem.

_note: neutral-drift-baseline absent: paper has no null model / Yule process / random-copying baseline — it is entirely about selection factors. replicator-ontology only weakly present: paper cites Dawkins/memes (Box 1, Glossary 'units of cultural transmission') but does NOT contest the discrete-copyable-unit assumption; it instead notes information is interpreted/transformed not copied (p.16), which leans against replicator ontology rather than engaging it as a defended premise — no confident anchoring quote.
